variants.title: Snakes smell the air with their tongues
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the tongue smell behind this animal behavior: Snakes smell the air with their tongues
variants.seo_title: Snakes smell the air with their tongues
variants.yt_description: Snakes smell the air with their tongues. Watch the tongue smell first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Snakes sample the air with a tongue flick.
variants.script: Snakes sample the air with a tongue flick. Watch the tongue flick, because it collects scent particles and sends them to a special organ in the mouth. That helps snakes track prey, mates, and danger without needing a loud chase. The air is information. Would you notice the trail?
variants.lead: Snakes sample the air with a tongue flick. Watch the tongue flick, because it collects scent particles and sends them to a special organ in the mouth. That helps snakes track prey, mates, and danger without needing a loud chase. The air is information. Would you notice the trail?

variants.title: Cats groom to reset their scent map
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the grooming signal behind this animal behavior: Cats use whiskers like built-in rulers
variants.seo_title: Cats groom to reset their scent map
variants.yt_description: Cats groom to reset their scent map. Watch the grooming signal first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Cats use grooming to reset their scent map.
variants.script: Cats use grooming to reset their scent map. Watch the tongue and paw movements, because grooming spreads familiar scent across the coat. variants.title: Storm clouds reveal a storm's heat engine
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the cloud spiral behind this animal behavior: Storm clouds reveal a storm's heat engine
variants.seo_title: Storm clouds reveal a storm's heat engine
variants.yt_description: Storm clouds reveal a storm's heat engine. Watch the cloud spiral first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Storm clouds show where a storm is feeding.
variants.script: Storm clouds show where a storm is feeding. Watch the spiral shape, because warm ocean air rises near the center and releases heat as clouds build. Rotation organizes that energy into bands. From above, the storm is not random; it is an engine. Which satellite clue next?
variants.lead: Storm clouds show where a storm is feeding. Watch the spiral shape, because warm ocean air rises near the center and releases heat as clouds build. Rotation organizes that energy into bands. From above, the storm is not random; it is an engine. Which satellite clue next?

variants.title: Butterflies taste flowers with their feet
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the taste feet behind this animal behavior: Butterflies taste flowers with their feet
variants.seo_title: Butterflies taste flowers with their feet
variants.yt_description: Butterflies taste flowers with their feet. Watch the taste feet first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Butterflies can taste a flower by landing on it.
variants.script: Butterflies can taste a flower by landing on it. Watch the feet touch first, because butterflies have taste sensors there. Landing helps them test whether a plant is food, a nursery, or a waste of energy. The meal check starts before the mouth even opens. Would you spot that?

variants.title: Deer aim their ears like sound radar
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the sound radar behind this animal behavior: Deer aim their ears like sound radar
variants.seo_title: Deer aim their ears like sound radar
variants.yt_description: Deer aim their ears like sound radar. Watch the sound radar first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Deer can aim each ear toward danger.
variants.script: Deer can aim each ear toward danger. Watch the ears first, because deer can turn them to sample sound from different directions. That quick listening check helps them decide whether to freeze, flee, or keep feeding. One quiet second tells the whole body what to do. Would you freeze?

variants.title: Lions use dark ear marks to guide cubs
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the ear marks behind this animal behavior: Lions use dark ear marks to guide cubs
variants.seo_title: Lions use dark ear marks to guide cubs
variants.yt_description: Lions use dark ear marks to guide cubs. Watch the ear marks first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Lions have dark ear marks cubs can follow.
variants.script: Lions have dark ear marks cubs can follow. Watch the backs of the ears, because those dark marks can stand out when a lion moves through grass. For cubs, that contrast helps track the adult ahead. The detail is small, but the pride depends on staying together. Would you spot it?

variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the human pointing behind this animal behavior: Dogs read the world through scent maps
variants.seo_title: Dogs read human pointing surprisingly well
variants.yt_description: Dogs read human pointing surprisingly well. Watch the human pointing first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Dogs can follow a human point fast.
variants.script: Dogs can follow a human point fast. Watch the eyes check the person, because dogs are unusually good at using human gestures.
